title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
content: New and specific disease-modifying therapies are being developed, and the FDA has recently approved some. For example, Aducanemab was approved by the FDA in June 2020. The FDA recently approved Lecanemab. Donanemab is also expected to receive FDA approval soon. These monoclonal antibodies act by removing amyloid from the brain as immunotherapy. Remternetug is another one of these promising amyloid-targeting immunotherapy drugs in the pipeline. [47] Aducanemab received accelerated FDA approval in June 2020. It was shown to reduce amyloid-beta plaque in the brain. However, it missed the primary phase III trial endpoint of clinical improvement. [48] Lecanemab received accelerated FDA approval in January 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain. The phase III trial showed a 27% slowing of disease progression. [49] Donanemab is expected to receive FDA approval in 2023. It reduced the amyloid-beta burden in the brain and slowed cognitive decline by 35%. [50]

title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Approved Indication
content: Aducanumab is approved to treat Alzheimer disease in patients who have MCI-AD or who are in the mild dementia stage of the disease. [11] [12] [11] The first iteration of the package insert described the indication simply as "Alzheimer disease," but the indication was given greater specificity within days of the accelerated approval. [7] Aducanumab has not been studied to date in individuals without cognitive impairment or in those who have progressed beyond the mild stage of the disease. Aducanumab's continued use and full approval will require further verification of its clinical benefits in post-approval studies.

title: Aducanumab -- Indications
content: Alzheimer disease is a progressive, chronic, neurodegenerative disease and the most common etiology of dementia. [1] In dementia patients aged 65 and older, Alzheimer disease accounts for two-thirds of the dementia cases, and it is the sixth most common cause of older adult mortality in the United States. [2] Alzheimer disease's characteristic hallmarks are extracellular neuritic plaques generated by the deposition of beta-amyloid protein and intracellular buildup of tau proteins, resulting in intracytoplasmic neurofibrillary tangles(NFT). [3] [4] [5]

title: Major Neurocognitive Disorder (Dementia) -- Treatment / Management
content: Aducanumab is a recombinant monoclonal antibody directed against amyloid beta that was recently approved by the FDA for the treatment of mild Alzheimer disease. Its approval remains highly controversial. The drug is costly and does not have clear, proven clinical benefits. It was approved based on positive clinical results seen in only 1 of the 2 phase III trials and aducanumab's effect on a surrogate endpoint (reducing amyloid beta plaques in the brain), which has not been proven clinically significant. [34] Lifestyle modifications to optimize cognitive function include optimizing sleep, eating an anti-inflammatory diet, adequate exercise, treating hearing or vision loss, minimizing stress, and maintaining normal blood sugar, cholesterol, and blood pressure levels. [35] Behavioral symptoms include irritability, anxiety, and depression. Antidepressants and sometimes antipsychotics can help with these symptoms. In addition, non-drug approaches like supportive care, memory training, physical exercise programs, and mental and social stimulation must be employed in symptom control. Patients and their families should be counseled about the disease and its consequences. They should be provided with all the necessary information about what to expect and how to react. Patients and their families should also be encouraged to seek social service consultations and to register with support groups and societies. Coaching caregivers on skills such as redirection and reassurance as opposed to repeated correction of patients confused due to dementia can avoid or de-escalate possible behavioral symptoms. Driving restrictions may have to be imposed.

title: Aducanumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Aducanumab was fast-tracked to FDA approval in 2021 and is indicated for use in patients with MCI-AD or the mild dementia stage of AD. The care of patients with AD requires management and supervision from an interprofessional healthcare team. The healthcare team should include a neurologist experienced in the diagnosis and management of dementia, working in close collaboration with the patient's primary care clinician and with access to vascular neurology and neuroradiologists experienced in evaluating amyloid PET and recognizing ARIA. Social work and pharmacy can provide useful support.  Routine care and follow-up for patients with AD by the interprofessional healthcare team promote efficient clinical evaluations and facilitate precise management plans and more reliable patient outcomes.

title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
content: In July 2021, the FDA granted accelerated approval for aducanumab; the decision was not based on the clinical data but rather on an intermediate outcome, namely the drug's ability to reduce the burden of excess β-amyloid in the brain and the attendant "reasonable" likelihood that amyloid reduction would benefit patients. [10] In conjunction with the approval, the FDA required that Biogen conduct a phase 4 clinical trial through 2030 to evaluate risks and benefits over the long term. Enrollment in the phase 4 trial was not a requirement for prescribing aducanumab.

title: Alzheimer Disease -- Treatment / Management -- Disease Modifying Therapies of AD
content: ARIA is a known side effect associated with amyloid immunotherapies. Apolipoprotein E4 allele and cerebral amyloid angiopathy findings in brain MRI are the most substantial risk factors for developing ARIA in patients treated with amyloid immunotherapies. In the Aducanemab phase I/II trials, ARIA-E and ARIA-H were present in 26.1% to 26.7% and 26.1% to 30.5% of the patients, respectively. Notably, the majority of these patients were asymptomatic. [50]

title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

By the time a patient is diagnosed with AD, the pathological process in the brain has been present for over a decade. title: Aducanumab -- Mechanism of Action
content: Within the neuritic plaques that characterize Alzheimer disease neuropathologically, β-amyloid exists in several forms, eg, as monomers, oligomers, protofibrils, and fibrils. Oligomers and protofibrils are neurotoxic. Therefore, removal of them by monoclonal antibody therapy is thought to be helpful in the treatment of Alzheimer disease. Soderberg et al characterized the binding properties of aducanumab, gantenerumab, and lecanemab on several forms of amyloid-beta via immunodepletion, inhibition ELISA, and surface plasmon resonance studies. [16] Aducanumab, gantenerumab, and lecanemab bind to monomer amyloid-beta with low affinity. Lecanemab had 10-fold greater binding to amyloid protofibrils when compared to fibrils. Aducanumab and gantenerumab are bound preferably to β-amyloid fibrils over protofibrils. Soderberg et al concluded that these monoclonal antibody binding differences may be important for therapeutic efficacy and adverse effects.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1714
content: Galantamin is classified as a parasympathomimetic agent, meaning it mimics the activity of the parasympathetic nervous system by enhancing cholinergic neurotransmission. As a member of the parasympathomimetic class, galantamin exerts its therapeutic effects primarily through reversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and allosteric modulation of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, actions that support cognitive function in conditions such as Alzheimer disease. Recognizing galantamin’s placement within the parasympathomimetic category helps clinicians understand its mechanism of action, potential side‑effect profile, and appropriate use in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 268
content: Anticholinesterase anti-dementia drugs are a specific subclass of anti‑dementia drugs that function as cholinesterase inhibitors, more precisely as inhibitors of acetylcholinesterase (referred to in the source language as “cholinesterasové inhibitory” and “inhibitory acetylcholinesteras”). Donepezil is a member of this anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug class. Anti‑dementia drugs as a whole also include other agents catalogued in the ATC system, such as memantin, which is listed as a member of the “Other anti‑dementia drugs in ATC” group. In some classification schemes, anti‑dementia drugs are placed under the broader psychotropic category of PSYCHOANALEPTICS; this category also encompasses psychostimulants and agents used for ADHD and nootropic purposes. Although anti‑dementia agents can be grouped within the larger psycho‑analeptic class for organizational purposes, they remain distinct therapeutic agents primarily indicated for the treatment of cognitive decline in dementia.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a type of anti‑dementia drug and function as cholinesterase inhibitors that block acetylcholinesterase activity. Donepezil is a specific member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug group. In addition to anticholinesterase agents, other anti‑dementia drugs in the ATC system include memantin, which is also a member of the broader anti‑dementia drug class. Both anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs and other anti‑dementia drugs fall under the larger category of psychoanaleptics, a class that is inversely related to psychostimulants and agents used for ADHD and nootropic purposes. Psychostimulants, in turn, are represented by the subclass of centrally acting sympathomimetics, which are defined as psychostimulants used for ADHD and nootropic effects. Centrally acting sympathomimetics include atomoxetine, methamfetamin (methamphetamine), methylfenidát (methylphenidate), and modafinil as individual members. Thus, the pharmacological landscape links anti‑dementia therapies—particularly cholinesterase‑inhibiting agents like donepezil and NMDA‑receptor antagonists like memantin—to the broader psychoanaleptic group, which shares classification overlap with central sympathomimetic psychostimulants employed in the treatment of attention‑deficit disorders and cognitive enhancement. This interconnected taxonomy aids clinicians in understanding the shared mechanisms and therapeutic categories across cognitive, stimulant, and neuropsychiatric drug classes.

title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 637
content: PSYCHOANALEPTICS is a broad pharmacological class that encompasses both anti‑dementia drugs and psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics. In this hierarchy, anti‑dementia drugs are considered a subclass of PSYCHOANALEPTICS, indicating that they share central nervous system‑stimulating properties with other members of the class. Within the anti‑dementia drug category, the ATC group “Other anti‑dementia drugs in ATC” is itself a specific subclass of anti‑dementia drugs, and memantin is listed as a member of this subgroup. Likewise, psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics, are classified under PSYCHOANALEPTICS, reflecting their role as central nervous system stimulants. This integrated classification highlights the relationships among cognitive enhancers, stimulants, and broader psychoanaleptic agents, aiding clinicians in understanding their shared mechanisms and therapeutic contexts.

content: Bromodiphenhydramine and pimavanserin are pharmacologic agents that may be employed in the treatment of Parkinson’s disease. Bromodiphenhydramine, an antihistamine with anticholinergic activity, has potential utility for alleviating motor symptoms associated with the neurodegenerative disorder. Pimavanserin, a selective serotonin‑5‑HT₂A inverse agonist, is also recognized for its possible therapeutic role in Parkinson’s disease, particularly for managing psychotic features and may provide broader symptomatic benefit. Recognizing both drugs as possible treatment options expands therapeutic considerations for clinicians managing patients with Parkinson’s disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 55
content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a type of anti‑dementia drug and are characterized by their cholinesterase‑inhibitory activity, specifically inhibition of acetylcholinesterase. Donepezil is a member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug group. Other anti‑dementia drugs in the ATC classification are also a type of anti‑dementia drug and include memantin as a member of this group. In some pharmacological taxonomies, the class PSYCHOANALEPTICS is linked to anti‑dementia drugs and to psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics through inverse “is‑a” relationships, suggesting an overlapping or alternative classification in which anti‑dementia agents and certain psychostimulants are considered subcategories of psychoanaleptics. Xanthine derivatives, psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics constitute a subclass of psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics, and include etofylline as an example. Propentofylline is identified as a member of this Xanthine‑derivative/psychostimulant group. The broader preparation category P [Preparations] serves as a parent class for both piretanide and propentofylline, indicating that these agents are grouped under the same preparatory classification. Overall, the knowledge base connects anti‑dementia therapies (both cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil and NMDA‑receptor antagonists like memantin) with broader pharmacological categories that may overlap with psychoanaleptic and psychostimulant classifications, and it situates specific agents such as propentofylline and piretanide within a shared preparation hierarchy.

title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 4 -- Community 2916
content: CTRP Agent Terminology is a classification system that encompasses a diverse group of pharmacologic agents, including naltrexone hydrochloride, gabapentin, ritonavir, cyproheptadine hydrochloride, haloperidol, neostigmin, and tanezumab. Naltrexone hydrochloride is an opioid receptor antagonist used to treat alcohol and opioid dependence. Gabapentin is an anticonvulsant and neuropathic pain medication. Ritonavir is a protease inhibitor employed in antiretroviral therapy for HIV infection. Cyproheptadine hydrochloride is an antihistamine with additional serotonin‑blocking properties, often used for allergic reactions and appetite stimulation. Haloperidol is a typical antipsychotic indicated for schizophrenia and acute psychosis. Neostigmin is an acetylcholinesterase inhibitor used to reverse neuromuscular blockade and treat myasthenia gravis. Tanezumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting nerve growth factor, investigated for the management of chronic pain. By grouping these agents together, CTRP Agent Terminology provides a structured reference for researchers and clinicians to identify and compare drugs across multiple therapeutic categories.

title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
content: Anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drugs are classified as a subtype of anti‑dementia drugs and are linked to cholinesterasové inhibitory through a broader relationship that reflects their mechanism of inhibiting acetylcholinesterase. The relationship “inhibitory acetylcholinesteras” further emphasizes that these agents act by blocking the enzyme acetylcholinesterase, thereby increasing central acetylcholine levels. Donepezil is a specific member of the anticholinesterase anti‑dementia drug group. In addition to this class, other anti‑dementia drugs in ATC, such as memantin, are also members of the broader anti‑dementia drug category, representing a distinct pharmacologic approach. Centrally acting sympathomimetics are identified as psychostimulants, agents used for ADHD and nootropics. This class includes atomoxetine, methylfenidát (methylphenidate), and modafinil, each listed as a member of the centrally acting sympathomimetic group. Their classification as psychostimulants underscores their role in enhancing central nervous system activity for therapeutic purposes in attention‑deficit disorders and cognitive enhancement. PSYCHOANALEPTICS are positioned as a separate category that is inversely related to both anti‑dementia drugs and the psychostimulant/ADHD/nootropic group, indicating that psycholeptic agents do not belong to either of these therapeutic classes. This distinction helps clarify that psycholeptics, which are primarily used for their sedative and antipsychotic effects, are not employed as anti‑dementia treatments nor as central stimulants.
